transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Skyler. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about some recent interactions I’ve had, especially with someone I know. It’s just been on my mind, and I’m not sure how to feel about it. It’s like, every time they reach out, I can’t help but feel a bit... disappointed, I guess? It's complicated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like these interactions have been weighing on you, and that disappointment is a significant feeling for you. Can you tell me a bit more about what specifically makes you feel disappointed when they reach out?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I suppose it’s just that it feels like they only get in touch when it’s convenient for them. Like, I’m not really valued in the same way. It’s hard not to think that way, you know? I mean, I want to believe there’s more to it, but it just... it stings. I end up withdrawing because I don’t want to deal with that feeling.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really painful to feel like you're not being valued in the same way, and that leads you to withdraw to protect yourself from that sting. It’s understandable to want to believe there’s more to the situation. When you think about those moments of withdrawal, what do you notice about how that affects you or your feelings toward this person?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: When I withdraw, it’s like I’m trying to shield myself from getting hurt again. But then, it just leaves me feeling... isolated, I guess. I think about them reaching out and I wonder if they even notice I’m gone. It’s a bit of a cycle, really. I pull away, and then I feel even more disappointed and alone. It’s frustrating because I want to connect, but I also don’t want to feel that hurt.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That cycle sounds really challenging—wanting to connect but also feeling the need to protect yourself from hurt. It makes sense that you would feel frustrated. When you think about that isolation, what thoughts come up for you about your relationship with this person or even about yourself in this situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I start to think that maybe I’m not important enough to them. Like, if they really cared, they wouldn’t just reach out when it suited them. It makes me question my worth, you know? I wonder if I’m just someone they keep around for convenience. It’s hard not to spiral into those thoughts, especially when I feel so alone in it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those thoughts really hit at the core of how you see your worth in this relationship. Feeling like you might be viewed as just a convenience can be deeply unsettling. It’s understandable that this would lead you to question your importance. When you find yourself spiraling into those thoughts, how do you usually cope with those feelings of loneliness and questioning your worth?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I tend to just pull back more, like I mentioned before. I think it’s easier to shut down than to confront those feelings head-on. Sometimes I distract myself with other things, like work or hobbies, but it doesn’t really solve anything. I guess I’m just trying to avoid the discomfort, but it doesn’t really help in the long run. I know I should probably talk about it more, but it’s tough to open up.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that pulling back feels like an easier option when facing those uncomfortable feelings. Distracting yourself can provide temporary relief, but it sounds like you’re aware that it doesn’t address the underlying issues. Opening up can be really challenging, especially when vulnerability is involved. What do you think holds you back the most from sharing your feelings with this person?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a mix of fear and uncertainty. I’m afraid of how they might react—what if they don’t care or think I’m overreacting? I don’t want to make things awkward or push them away even more. Plus, I worry that if I open up, it’ll just confirm my fears about not being valued. It’s like, the risk feels too high, so I just keep it all bottled up.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That fear of how they might react and the uncertainty about their feelings can be really paralyzing. It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to express yourself and the worry that it could lead to more hurt or awkwardness. It’s understandable to want to protect yourself from that risk. When you think about those fears, what do you think would help you feel more secure in expressing your feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think if I felt more certain that they genuinely cared about me, it might make it easier to open up. Maybe if there was more consistent communication or reassurance from them, I wouldn’t feel so vulnerable sharing my thoughts. But it’s hard to ask for that when I’m already feeling unsure. I guess I just wish I could trust that it would be received well, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having that reassurance and consistent communication would really help you feel more secure in opening up. Trusting that your feelings would be received well is such an important part of feeling safe in a relationship. It’s understandable to feel hesitant to ask for what you need when you’re already feeling unsure. What do you think might help you build that trust or create a space for more open communication with them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe starting with smaller conversations could help? Like, just sharing little things and seeing how they respond. If they seem receptive, it might give me the confidence to share more. I think I also need to remind myself that it’s okay to express my needs, even if it feels risky. But it’s still daunting to think about taking that first step. I guess I just need to find a way to feel safe doing it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting with smaller conversations sounds like a thoughtful approach. It could help you gauge their receptiveness and build your confidence gradually. It’s great that you recognize the importance of expressing your needs, even when it feels risky. Finding a way to feel safe in that process is key. What do you think would help create that sense of safety for you as you consider taking that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having a clear idea of what I want to say might help. If I can organize my thoughts beforehand, it might feel less overwhelming. Also, maybe having a supportive friend to talk to about it first could give me a little boost. Just knowing that I’m not alone in this might make it easier to take that leap. I suppose it’s about finding a balance between being honest and protecting myself, which is tricky.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Having a clear idea of what you want to say and talking it through with a supportive friend sounds like a solid plan. It’s understandable that finding that balance between honesty and self-protection feels tricky, but it seems like you’re approaching it thoughtfully. It’s okay to take your time with this process. How do you feel about the idea of reaching out to that friend for support as you prepare to express your thoughts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reaching out to a friend could be really helpful. Just having someone to bounce ideas off of and share my feelings with might ease some of that pressure. I feel a bit nervous about it, though—what if they don’t understand? But I guess it’s worth a try. It might help me feel less alone in this, and maybe they could offer some perspective that I’m missing.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel nervous about reaching out, especially when you’re unsure how someone might respond. But it sounds like you recognize the potential benefits of sharing your feelings with a friend, and that’s a positive step. It’s okay to take that leap, even if it feels a bit daunting. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable when you reach out to them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would help to remind myself that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that my feelings are valid. Maybe I could also prepare a few key points to share so I don’t feel too scattered when I talk to them. It might make the conversation feel more manageable. And just knowing that they’re a friend who cares could give me some comfort. I guess I just need to focus on the support I might receive rather than the fear of how it could go.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are really insightful strategies. Reminding yourself that your feelings are valid and preparing key points can definitely help make the conversation feel more manageable. Focusing on the support you might receive from your friend is a positive shift in perspective. It sounds like you’re taking thoughtful steps to navigate this situation. As you think about reaching out, what’s one small action you could take in the next few days to move forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start by sending a text to my friend just to check in. It could be a low-pressure way to reconnect and see how they’re doing. From there, if the conversation flows well, I could gradually bring up what I’ve been feeling. It feels like a small step, but it might help me ease into it. I’ll try to remind myself that it’s okay to take my time with this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan! Sending a text to check in is a wonderful way to reconnect without too much pressure. It gives you the opportunity to gauge how the conversation goes and gradually share your feelings. Taking your time with this process is important, and it’s great that you’re allowing yourself that space. How do you feel about taking that first step to reach out?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of nervousness and a bit of relief, honestly. It’s daunting to put myself out there, but at the same time, I think it could be a positive step. I know I need to start somewhere, and this feels manageable. I just hope it goes well, but I guess I’ll have to see how it unfolds. It’s nice to think that I might be able to share what I’ve been feeling, even if it’s just a little at first.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely normal to feel a mix of nervousness and relief as you prepare to take this step. Acknowledging that it’s manageable is a positive mindset. It’s great that you’re open to seeing how things unfold, and even starting with a little can lead to meaningful conversations. Remember, it’s okay to take things at your own pace. As you move forward, how do you plan to take care of yourself through this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll try to be gentle with myself and not put too much pressure on how the conversation goes. Maybe I’ll set aside some time to do things I enjoy, like reading or going for a walk, to help manage any anxiety that comes up. I might also remind myself that it’s okay to feel a bit vulnerable and that it doesn’t define my worth. Just taking care of my emotional space will be important as I navigate this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful approach. Being gentle with yourself and allowing space for activities you enjoy can really help manage any anxiety. Reminding yourself that vulnerability doesn’t define your worth is such an important perspective. It’s clear you’re being thoughtful about your emotional well-being as you navigate this. As you prepare to reach out and take care of yourself, is there anything else you’d like to explore or reflect on before we wrap up today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve covered a lot of what’s been on my mind. It feels good to talk about it and have a bit of clarity on my next steps. I guess I just want to make sure I remember to be patient with myself as I go through this process. It’s nice to have a space to reflect on it all. Thank you for that.
